frame_analyses.description: Setup view — Penrose drain on red foam suture block. A roll of Microfoam tape visible in upper right corner of frame (not part of task). Standard FLS Task 5 configuration.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Standard setup. Slightly different camera angle than previous videos.

frame_analyses.description: At 10 seconds, both instruments in field near drain with needle and suture visible. Quick entry consistent with trained performance.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Fast instrument entry. Needle engaged promptly.

frame_analyses.description: At 40 seconds, needle visible held by right instrument above drain. Suture thread extending downward. Left instrument working near drain surface.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Still in suture placement at 40 seconds. Needle driving in progress.

frame_analyses.description: At 91 seconds, suture wrapping visible. Right instrument has suture looped around it. Left instrument positioned to hold free end. Wrapping for first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First throw starting at ~85-90 seconds is significantly slower than post-2 (58s) and comparable to the pre-training timing (85s). Extended suture placement phase consumed most of the extra time.

frame_analyses.description: At 131 seconds, both instruments working near drain with suture. Instrument positions suggest second or third throw in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Throw phases are proceeding. The instrument positions suggest wrapping/tightening.

frame_analyses.description: At 161 seconds, both instruments visible with suture loop between them above the drain. Active wrapping or tightening of the third throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Still in throw phases at 161 seconds. This is significantly later in the video than previous post-training attempts reached the cutting phase.

frame_analyses.description: At 181 seconds, SCISSORS clearly visible (ring handles in lower right). Left instrument holding suture near drain. Cutting in progress. Drain slit shows a visible gap at this point.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting phase beginning at ~175s. Scissors ring handles are clearly identifiable in this frame — first time scissors are unambiguously visible across all videos.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 201 seconds. Instruments withdrawn. Completed knot visible on Penrose drain with dark suture thread. The drain slit shows a VISIBLE GAP — the upper portion is not approximated. One suture tail extending upward appears somewhat long. Drain remains attached to suture block (not avulsed).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Final result shows partial slit closure with a gap similar to pre-training and post-3 results. The suture tail length appears longer than ideal, suggesting cutting may not have been as close to the knot. This is the slowest post-training attempt at ~196 seconds.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 10 shows wrapping around right instrument. Double-loop pattern suggests surgeon's knot. Confidence moderate.
Frame 14 shows throw in progress. Confidence low on hand determination.
Third throw completed but final result shows visible gap in drain slit. The gap suggests insufficient knot tension overall. Confidence low.

technique_summary: This sixth video represents the most significant regression in the training series — at 196 seconds with a visible drain gap, performance has returned to near pre-training baseline levels (pre: 198s/389.5). The primary time loss is in suture placement, which took approximately 62 seconds — the longest of any attempt. The throw phases (30s, 28s, 34s) are slower than the improvements seen in post-3 (22-27s). The cutting phase at 24 seconds is an improvement over post-4's 44 seconds but still above the post-2 benchmark of 18 seconds. The visible gap in the final result suggests knot tension issues that were resolved in post-2 and post-4 have returned. Needle loading remains efficient at 13 seconds. This pattern of variable performance with regression from a peak (post-2) suggests the trainee may benefit from more deliberate, focused practice on the specific sub-skills that are regressing: suture placement speed and knot tension.
improvement_suggestions: CRITICAL: Suture placement has regressed to 62 seconds — this is worse than any previous attempt except the very first. Practice dedicated needle driving drills outside of the full task to rebuild speed
Knot tension has regressed — the visible gap suggests throws are not being tightened sufficiently. Practice individual throw tightening with focus on pulling both ends firmly before releasing
Consider fatigue as a factor — if these attempts were done in the same session, muscle fatigue and mental fatigue could explain the regression pattern
Review the post-2 video (best attempt) and identify what was different about the approach that session vs this one
The throw phases have also slowed (30s, 28s, 34s vs 22-27s in post-3) — practice wrapping speed
strengths: Needle loading remains efficient at 13 seconds — this skill is solidly acquired
All three throws still completed in correct sequence
Drain not avulsed — tissue handling remains appropriate
Cutting phase (24s) improved from post-4's problematic 44 seconds

key_observations: Suture placement worst ever: 62s (pre was 50s, post-2 was 40s)
Overall time nearly matches pre-training baseline (196s vs 198s)
Drain gap returned — partial closure like pre-training
Cutting improved from post-4 (24s vs 44s) but still not at post-2 level (18s)
Needle loading stable at 13s — only fully retained improvement
Score of 391.5 is essentially pre-training level (389.5)
Fatigue or loss of technique consistency likely factors
